result:
All done. Here's what changed and why:

---

## What the refactor did

### Before (1 file → 168 lines doing everything)
```
expense_cli.py   # I/O, entry construction, CLI parsing, formatting — all interleaved
```

### After (3 focused modules)
```
expense_cli/
  __init__.py    # package docstring
  __main__.py    # python -m expense_cli entry point
  storage.py     # EXPENSES_FILE, load(), save()
  models.py      # create_expense() — pure logic, no I/O
  cli.py         # cmd_add/list/summary + argparse wiring
```

### How each piece was extracted

| Module | From | Key change |
|---|---|---|
| **`storage.py`** | `_load()`, `_save()`, `EXPENSES_FILE` | Dropped the underscore prefixes — these are now the public API of their own module, not private helpers. |
| **`models.py`** | The dict-building block inside `cmd_add` (lines 40–49) | Pulled into `create_expense()`. Validation moved from `sys.exit()` (a UI concern) to `raise ValueError` (a domain concern). `cli.py` catches it and does the `sys.exit`. |
| **`cli.py`** | Everything else | Imports `storage` and `models` instead of reaching into its own globals. `cmd_add` is now 8 lines instead of 16 — it delegates construction and I/O. |

### Test changes — minimal and mechanical

Only three lines changed per occurrence, all following the same pattern:

```python
# Before                                    # After
import expense_cli                          from expense_cli import cli, storage
monkeypatch.setattr(expense_cli, ...)       monkeypatch.setattr(storage, ...)
expense_cli.cmd_add(...)                    cli.cmd_add(...)
```

The fixture now targets `storage.EXPENSES_FILE` directly — the actual module that reads the variable — instead of patching it on the top-level namespace. All 20 tests pass with identical assertions.
